food_name: Kopi (Singapore Coffee with Sweetened Condensed Milk, Hot)

local_names.malay: Kopi (Kopi dengan Susu Pekat Manis, Panas)
local_names.hindi: कोपी (मीठे गाढ़े दूध के साथ सिंगापुर कॉफ़ी)

aliases: कोपी (मीठे गाढ़े दूध के साथ सिंगापुर कॉफ़ी)

hindi: कोपी (मीठे गाढ़े दूध के साथ सिंगापुर कॉफ़ी)

food_subgroup: Hot coffee beverage with sweetened condensed milk; Nanyang-style Robusta coffee brewed through cloth sock filter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic coffee beverage category (FOODON_03306174) but no specific entry for Singapore Kopi (Robusta coffee with sweetened condensed milk, Nanyang-style preparation). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Coffee beverage — used as closest parent for Singapore Kopi (Robusta with sweetened condensed milk, hot)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Singapore Kopi. A0KZJ (coffee beverage) used as closest defensible parent. Specific Nanyang-style preparation with condensed milk is not represented in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Singapore Kopi as a specific food product. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual beverage preparations at this specificity.

preparation_method: brewed through cloth sock filter (kopi sock)
pulled between tin pots to aerate
served hot over sweetened condensed milk
main_ingredients: Robusta coffee beans (Nanyang-style roasted with sugar and margarine/butter)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard kopitiam cup of Kopi is approximately 220 ml (220 g), delivering roughly 114 kcal, 19.4 g carbohydrate, and 15–22 g sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Kopi (condensed milk coffee) — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. The Kopi entry (condensed milk coffee) is an exact match for this record. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry. Micronutrients with lower confidence (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Coffee Beverages with Condensed Milk — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore Kopi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~84 is derived from the high-GI profile of sweetened condensed milk (GI ~61 for condensed milk itself, but the overall beverage GI is elevated by the high sucrose content and rapid absorption). The GI range of 80–90 for condensed-milk-sweetened coffee beverages is consistent with published values for high-sucrose dairy-coffee drinks. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (84 × 20.24) / 100 ≈ 17.0; rounded to 16.8 using 220g serving (9.2g carb/100g × 2.2 = 20.24g carb per cup). Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Caffeine content and moderate sodium from condensed milk
hypertension.guidance: A standard 220 ml cup of Kopi contains approximately 84 mg sodium and 100–150 mg caffeine (Robusta); habitual caffeine consumers develop tolerance to the transient systolic blood pressure rise, but patients with uncontrolled hypertension should limit intake to one cup per day and consider switching to Kopi-O (black coffee) to reduce both sodium and sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar load from sweetened condensed milk (15–22 g sugar per cup, GI ~84, GL ~16.8 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be strongly counselled to avoid standard Kopi; two cups daily contributes 30–44 g hidden sugar from beverages alone — often unreported — and clinicians should systematically ask about kopitiam beverage habits; recommended substitution tiers are Kopi Siu Dai (reduced condensed milk), Kopi-C (evaporated milk with less sugar), Kopi-O Siu Dai (minimal sugar), or Kopi-O Kosong (no sugar).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from condensed milk and high sugar driving dyslipidaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each cup of Kopi provides approximately 2.2 g saturated fat and 19.4 g carbohydrate; regular daily consumption contributes to elevated triglycerides and LDL cholesterol over time, and patients with established cardiovascular disease should limit to one cup per day or switch to lower-sugar variants such as Kopi-O Kosong, while noting that coffee polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) in Robusta beans have documented anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective associations in epidemiological studies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coffee polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) with anti-inflammatory properties offset by high sugar
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Robusta coffee contains significantly higher chlorogenic acid content than Arabica, and regular coffee consumption is associated with reduced systemic inflammation markers; however, the high sugar content (8.8 g/100 g) may drive glycaemic-inflammatory pathways, so patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider lower-sugar Kopi variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kopi (咖啡) is Singapore's most iconic and culturally embedded beverage — the heartbeat of the kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) and a daily ritual for millions of Singaporeans and Malaysians. It is fundamentally different from Western-style coffee in both its beans and its preparation. The coffee uses Robusta beans (higher caffeine, more bitter, and more robust than Arabica) roasted Nanyang-style: the beans are tumbled in a wok with sugar and butter or margarine, producing a caramelised, slightly sticky, intensely aromatic roast unique to Southeast Asia. Brewing is done using a 'kopi sock' — a cloth flannel filter — through which boiling water is passed to produce a dense, syrupy concentrate. The concentrate is then 'pulled' between two large tin pots to aerate it and achieve the characteristic frothy top. The standard Kopi is served with a generous measure of sweetened condensed milk deposited at the bottom of the cup; the hot coffee concentrate is poured over and stirred, dissolving the condensed milk into a thick, creamy, intensely sweet and bitter-balanced cup. The flavour profile is unique: more robust and smoky than Western café coffee, but rounded by the condensed milk into a distinctive richness. Nutritionally, a standard 220 ml cup delivers approximately 114 kcal, 19.4 g carbohydrate, and 15–22 g sugar — almost entirely from the condensed milk. This sugar is routinely unreported by patients who perceive kopi as a beverage rather than a food. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes who drink kopi twice daily are consuming 30–44 g hidden sugar per day from beverages alone. Clinicians in Singapore must systematically ask about kopitiam beverage habits. The kopitiam counselling tiers for diabetes management are: (1) Kopi Siu Dai — reduced condensed milk; (2) Kopi-C — evaporated milk with sugar; (3) Kopi-O Siu Dai — black coffee with minimal sugar; (4) Kopi-O Kosong — black coffee, no sugar. GI is approximately 84 (high), GL per cup approximately 16.8.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes, with an exact match for Kopi (condensed milk coffee). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous evidence at confidence 0.9. Three micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible closest-parent matches with honest gap notes.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; this is expected for a liquid coffee-milk beverage with no plant fibre components. Trans fat is 0.0 g — confirmed trace/negligible per source. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry and are set to -1.0. Water content is not reported by the source and is set to -1.0. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) as no direct GI measurement for Singapore Kopi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; the estimate is based on the high-sucrose condensed milk composition and published GI ranges for similar sweetened dairy-coffee beverages. Portions: the default serving is 220 g (1 standard kopitiam cup); a 300 g large cup and 250 ml serving size are also documented. The duplicate 250 ml portion entry in the input has been deduplicated. FoodEx2 code A0KZJ and FoodOn URI FOODON_03306174 are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for Singapore Kopi. SNOMED CT has no applicable food product code at this specificity.
